messages.content:

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
    "opinion": "I've been an electrician in Washington DC for over forty years, so I'm used to problem-solving. But keeping up with the latest on COVID vaccines? That's a whole different ball game. My dad has a PhD, but he doesn't say much about these topics—usually busy with his research or traveling internationally as part of his job. My mom only went to upper secondary school and she prefers talking about gardening or maybe helping me fix something at home.

When the pandemic first hit, I saw lots of misinformation on social media, spending several hours a day scrolling through it all made my head hurt sometimes. But being an immigrant Muslim family man with normal psychological features keeps things in perspective for me. We've always valued education and taking care of our health since we came here from another country back when my father was young.

So managing discussions about healthcare requires patience, like how I handle tricky wires at work—don't jump to conclusions too fast. First step? Listen carefully without interrupting because people need respect even if they don't understand vaccines properly yet.

I believe in getting facts straight from reliable sources when these conversations pop up online or with friends during board game sessions after work. For example, the CDC has clear info about COVID-19 safety measures and vaccine effectiveness—things my primary school education didn't cover but are important now more than ever.

My agreeableness score is pretty high (88 out of 100) meaning I'm generally cooperative in conversations—but sometimes folks get emotional talking vaccines these days. In those cases, it's best to calmly explain things step by step like fixing a faulty connection rather than being confrontational which could make them defensive.

Also consider my neuroticism moderately low: even though topics can worry me slightly especially with how much time we spend online these days—the world of social media debates—staying relatively calm helps keep discussions productive. I don't want unnecessary arguments disrupting family life or work conversations.
",
    "reasoning_summary": "As an immigrant Muslim man, I value education and health but rely on external sources for complex topics like vaccines since my formal schooling was limited to primary level. High agreeableness leads me to prefer cooperative approaches over confrontation in discussions. Moderate openness means I'm open to learning new things if explained clearly without feeling threatened by change.",
    "tone": "Calm, respectful and patient with a touch of everyday wisdom from years as an electrician",
    "citations": ["CDC"],
    "safety_notes": "",
    "used_persona_fields":["age","gender","sexual_orientation","religious_beliefs","time_onsocialmedia","hobbies","ocean"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been an electrician in Washington DC for over forty years, so I'm used to problem-solving. But keeping up with the latest on COVID vaccines? That's a whole different ball game. My dad has a PhD, but he doesn't say much about these topics—usually busy with his research or traveling internationally as part of his job. My mom only went to upper secondary school and she prefers talking about gardening or maybe helping me fix something at home.

When the pandemic first hit, I saw lots of misinformation on social media, spending several hours a day scrolling through it all made my head hurt sometimes. But being an immigrant Muslim family man with normal psychological features keeps things in perspective for me. We've always valued education and taking care of our health since we came here from another country back when my father was young.

So managing discussions about healthcare requires patience, like how I handle tricky wires at work—don't jump to conclusions too fast. First step? Listen carefully without interrupting because people need respect even if they don't understand vaccines properly yet.

I believe in getting facts straight from reliable sources when these conversations pop up online or with friends during board game sessions after work. For example, the CDC has clear info about COVID-19 safety measures and vaccine effectiveness—things my primary school education didn't cover but are important now more than ever.

My agreeableness score is pretty high (88 out of 100) meaning I'm generally cooperative in conversations—but sometimes folks get emotional talking vaccines these days. In those cases, it's best to calmly explain things step by step like fixing a faulty connection rather than being confrontational which could make them defensive.

Also consider my neuroticism moderately low: even though topics can worry me slightly especially with how much time we spend online these days—the world of social media debates—staying relatively calm helps keep discussions productive. I don't want unnecessary arguments disrupting family life or work conversations.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: As an immigrant Muslim man, I value education and health but rely on external sources for complex topics like vaccines since my formal schooling was limited to primary level. High agreeableness leads me to prefer cooperative approaches over confrontation in discussions. Moderate openness means I'm open to learning new things if explained clearly without feeling threatened by change.
response_parsed.tone: Calm, respectful and patient with a touch of everyday wisdom from years as an electrician
